definition: A computational model made of nodes and connections that fits curved lines (squiggles) to data, enabling predictions on new inputs.
A description for neural networks where the internal workings are hard to interpret — this series aims to open the box and explain each step.
The algorithm used to fit (train) a neural network to data by estimating the best parameter values. Covered in depth in Part 2 of the series.
The numerical values on each connection in a neural network — analogous to the slope and intercept of a line — estimated during training.
The bent or curved line inside each hidden-layer node that allows the network to fit non-linear (squiggly) patterns in data.
A smooth, curved activation function used in this StatQuest. Its output is computed as log(1 + e^x), producing a gentle curve.
Rectified Linear Unit — a piecewise-linear activation function that outputs 0 for negative inputs and the input value itself for positive inputs.
An S-shaped activation function that squishes any input into a value between 0 and 1. Common in early neural network tutorials.
The node where raw data (e.g., dosage) enters the network. A network can have one or many input nodes.
The final node that produces the network's prediction (e.g., predicted effectiveness). A network can have one or many output nodes.
Any layer of nodes between the input and output nodes. These layers do the heavy lifting of reshaping activation functions to fit the data.
Neural networks with many hidden layers, giving them the capacity to learn highly complex, multi-level patterns from data.
Written as log or ln — the logarithm with base e (≈2.718). Used in activation functions like Soft Plus: log(1 + e^x).
The value fed into an activation function, calculated by multiplying the input by a weight and adding a bias before entering the curved function.
The parameters that multiply values along connections in a neural network, controlling how much each input influences the output.
The parameters that are added (not multiplied) along connections, allowing the network to shift its activation functions left or right.
Josh Starmer's term for the non-linear curve a neural network fits to data — made by reshaping and adding activation functions together.
Multiplying the y-axis values of an activation function output by a weight, which stretches or flips the curve into a new shape.
